summary:
The perfect example of a tooth transitioning from an anterior position, such as incisors, towards more posterior positions like premolars and molars. This shift occurs during normal dental development where front-facing teeth move back into chewing functions over time.
To further clarify this point, consider how central or lateral incisors gradually give way to canines which then progress toward first and second molar locations throughout childhood and adolescence. **Summary:** 
A classic example of the transition from anterior to posterior positioning involves the movement of deciduous incisors giving way to permanent canine teeth, which eventually yield space for the eruption of premolars and molars. During dental growth and development, front-teeth progressively migrate backward to support essential jaw function and occlusion.
